source_paper_title: Nash Equilibria for Dividend Distribution with Competition

Page 23, Remark 4.9 (end of Section 4, after Theorem 4.1).

The question as to whether an equilibrium exists in which Player 1 uses a strategy and Player 2 uses a control (both either pure or randomised) is open when \(y > x\), especially if we seek an equilibrium valid for all parameter choices and initial states.
title: Existence of reversed role equilibrium in dividend competition game with asymmetry
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-time two-player stochastic game in which each player controls her own cash-reserve process by paying dividends. Let (B_t)_{t\ge 0} be a standard one-dimensional Brownian motion on a filtered probability space \((\Omega,\mathcal F,(\mathcal F_t)_{t\ge0},\mathbb P)\). Fix parameters \(\mu_0>0\) (duopoly drift), \(\hat\mu>\mu_0\) (monopoly drift), \(\sigma>0\) (volatility), and \(r>0\) (discount rate).

For initial endowments \((x,y)\in[0,\infty)^2\), admissible dividend controls are nondecreasing, right-continuous, \((\mathcal F_t)\)-adapted processes \(L=(L_t)_{t\ge0}\), \(D=(D_t)_{t\ge0}\) with \(L_{0-}=D_{0-}=0\), and such that at any time the jump in dividends does not exceed the pre-jump cash reserve. The controlled cash reserves are
\[
X_t = x + \mu_0 t + \sigma B_t - L_t,\qquad Y_t = y + \mu_0 t + \sigma B_t - D_t.
\]
Default times are
\[
\gamma_X = \inf\{t\ge0: X_t\le 0\},\qquad \gamma_Y = \inf\{t\ge0: Y_t\le 0\}.
\]
If one firm defaults first, the surviving firm becomes a monopolist and receives a continuation value given by the value function \(\hat v\) of the classical single-firm dividend problem with drift \(\hat\mu\):
\[
\hat v(z) = \sup_{\xi}\,\mathbb E_z\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_C]} e^{-rt}\,d\xi_t\Big],\qquad C_t = z + \hat\mu t + \sigma B_t - \xi_t,\quad \gamma_C=\inf\{t\ge0:C_t\le 0\},
\]
where the supremum is over admissible dividend controls \(\xi\).

Player payoffs for a control pair \((L,D)\) are
\[
J^1_{x,y}(L,D)=\mathbb E\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_X\wedge\gamma_Y]} e^{-rt}\,dL_t + \mathbf 1_{\{\gamma_Y<\gamma_X\}} e^{-r\gamma_Y}\,\hat v(X_{\gamma_Y})\Big],
\]
\[
J^2_{x,y}(D,L)=\mathbb E\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_X\wedge\gamma_Y]} e^{-rt}\,dD_t + \mathbf 1_{\{\gamma_X<\gamma_Y\}} e^{-r\gamma_X}\,\hat v(Y_{\gamma_X})\Big].
\]

Strategies are (possibly randomised) feedback maps that select dividend controls based on the observed Brownian path and the opponent's past dividend payments; a Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile in which no player can improve her payoff by unilateral deviation within the admissible strategy class.

The paper constructs, for the asymmetric initial endowment case \(y>x\), a Nash equilibrium in which Player 1 uses a pure control (depending only on the Brownian path) and Player 2 uses a (pure) feedback strategy that reacts to Player 1's control. The paper explicitly notes that it is not known whether an equilibrium exists with the roles reversed: Player 1 using a strategy and Player 2 using a control.
problem_definition_latex:
For asymmetric initial endowments \(y>x\), determine whether there exists a Nash equilibrium (in the sense of feedback strategies allowing randomisation) in which Player 1 uses a strategy that can depend on the opponent's dividend path, while Player 2 uses a control depending only on the Brownian path.

Equivalently: decide whether there exists an equilibrium strategy profile \((\Xi_1,\Xi_2)\) for the game starting from \((x,y)\) with \(y>x\) such that Player 1's action is implemented via a strategy map that may depend on the opponent's cumulative dividends, whereas Player 2's action is implemented via a control (i.e., a dividend process) that does not depend on observing the opponent's dividends. The equilibrium should be valid for all parameter choices \((\mu_0,\hat\mu,\sigma,r)\) and all initial states with \(y>x\), as posed in the paper.
latex:

Let (B_t)_{t\ge 0} be a standard one-dimensional Brownian motion on a filtered probability space \((\Omega,\mathcal F,(\mathcal F_t)_{t\ge0},\mathbb P)\). For asymmetric initial endowments \(y>x\), determine whether there exists a Nash equilibrium (in the sense of feedback strategies allowing randomisation) in which Player 1 uses a strategy that can depend on the opponent's dividend path, while Player 2 uses a control depending only on the Brownian path.

location_in_paper: Page 23, Remark 4.9 (end of Section 4, after Theorem 4.1).
open_problem_quote: The question as to whether an equilibrium exists in which Player 1 uses a strategy and Player 2 uses a control (both either pure or randomised) is open when \(y > x\), especially if we seek an equilibrium valid for all parameter choices and initial states.
subject_classification: stochastic differential games and singular control
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is existential and involves an infinite-dimensional strategy space (feedback maps with fixed-point well-posedness constraints), so there is no obvious finite or efficiently enumerable instance space for automated counterexample search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Evaluating candidate equilibria would require solving coupled controlled SDE fixed-point problems and verifying best-response inequalities over broad strategy classes, which does not yield a straightforward automated fitness signal suitable for evolutionary code search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
